header: Head of B2B Unit / Quinto Andar

lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- Data Storytelling -- Making Numbers Alive
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eduardo Chiba | Aula 12 -- Data Storytelling

listenings.text: Let me walk you through the numbers on this chart. As you can see, revenue rose sharply in the first quarter, from forty million to sixty-five million. That is the highest figure in our history. After that, growth was steady through the second quarter, with a small dip in April. The most important point is the trend: over the full year, our revenue grew by almost fifty percent, which is far stronger than the market average. The blue line shows our company, and the gray line shows our main competitor. Notice that the gap between the two lines is much wider than last year. In short, we are not only growing, we are growing faster than everyone else in the segment.

Okay, let me rehearse the data part of my talk one more time. I want to make the numbers tell a story, not just read them. So, instead of saying the revenue was sixty-five million, I will say revenue rose sharply to a record sixty-five million. I should compare, too. Our growth was twice as fast as the market, and our cost per lead was much lower than last year. I think I am using too many figures on one slide. The audience cannot follow ten numbers at once. I need to highlight one key figure per slide and let the trend do the talking. Slower, fewer numbers, one clear message each time. That is how you make data come alive.

extra_audio.text: First, you show the chart and tell the audience what they are looking at. Then you point to the overall trend, so people see the big picture. After that, you highlight the single most important figure on the slide. Next, you compare it with last year or with the market to give it meaning. Finally, you sum up the story the numbers tell in one clear sentence.
